===Designated cultural property===

===Nakatsugawa City Designated Cultural Property===
*Pair of wooden guardian dogs<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-06 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E7%8B%9B%E7%8A%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%AF%BE |title=Pair of wooden guardian dogs |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 55cm Kamakura period Designated date: March 24, 1968

It is a guardian dog facing sideways, and the coloring has faded. Since there is no inscription, the author and year are unknown. Since it is made of cypress wood parquet, it is estimated to have been made at the end of the Kamakura period.
*Wooden Goshintai Statue<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-04 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E5%BE%A1%E7%A5%9E%E4%BD%93%E5%83%8F |title=Wooden Goshintai Statue |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 20-33cm Muromachi period Designated date: March 24, 1968

Of the 11 statues of gods and 2 statues of monks, there are 6 statues of gods holding scepters, 10 statues with robes, and 9 statues with beards. All of the carvings are made of wood (single carving), and most of them are tall in shape. There are about six statues with an inscription dated ``1352'' on the bottom of the statue.

===Nakatsugawa City designated natural monument===
*Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-03-03 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%81%AE%E3%83%92%E3%83%88%E3%83%84%E3%83%90%E3%82%BF%E3%82%B4 |title=Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>

Eye circumference 1m, 0.84m Tree height 22m Designation date: October 28, 1971

{{ill|lt=Hitotsubatago|Q1073191|WD=Q1073191|1=Chionanthus retusus}} is a plant of the {{ill|lt=Oleander|Q1209922|WD=Q1209922|1=Osmanthus fragrans}} family, and its distribution in Japan is only in the middle reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}} and {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051|1=Tsushima Island}}. The four-lobed, thin, white flowers bloom around May, and when in full bloom they look like snow. Because it is a very rare and unfamiliar tree, it is also called the ``Nanjamonja tree''. This tree is divided into two trunks at the base.

===Surrounding Ruins===
There are many kiln ruins from the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period on the hill north of the Hachimangu forest of Sakamoto Shrine, some of which have been designated as historic sites by Gifu Prefecture.

Fragments of white porcelain pottery (yamachawan) from the Kamakura and Muromachi periods have been collected over a wide area, including this area.

In addition, the Five Ring Pagoda and the Hokyoin Pagoda can be seen in various places, and although it is located at a distance from Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the ruins of a medieval residence have been excavated from the Nishikagai Ruins, and related artifacts have also been unearthed.

=== Transportation ===
* About 2km from {{ill|lt=Chuo Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Minosakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}}
* {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kotsu|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|lt=Kitaena Bus|1=Kitaena Kotsu}}Get off at Sakamoto Misaka Line "Sendanbayashi" bus stop, then walk for 10 minutes
: This is different from the "Nakatsugawa Sendanbayashi Bus Stop" for express buses.

{{nihongo|'''Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu'''|坂本神社八幡宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mino Province]].

'''Hachimanjinja'' (Hachimanjinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa City|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}{{ill|lt=Sendanbayashi Village|Q22131707|WD=Q22131707|lt=Sendanbayashi|1=Sendanbayashi}}.

=== Enshrined God ===
*{{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
*{{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Obichuhiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
*{{ill|lt=Hachimangami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda Betsu no Mikoto|1=Hachiman}}

=== History ===
Originally, it was a place where {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} was enshrined to pray for safety on the {{ill|lt=Kanzaka Pass|Q11588847|WD=Q11588847|1=Misaka Pass}}.

It is said that in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the shrine was built with a solicitation from {{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}.

{{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} Sakamoto Shrine, {{ill|lt=Nakagawa Shrine (Nakatsugawa City)|Q11364920|WD=Q11364920|lt=Nakagawa Shrine|1=Nakagawa Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}} are listed.

Although the period is unknown, {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}{{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Hachimangu|1=Usa Jingū}} was promoted and enshrined there. It is said that around this time the name was changed to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

In the Heian period, {{ill|lt=Honji Suijaku|Q3080781|WD=Q3080781|1=Honji suijaku}} led to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=a mixture of Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was considered to be the same as {{ill|lt=Amida Nyorai|Q236242|WD=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}, and shrine priests controlled Shinto rituals.In {{ill|lt=Kanno|Q1145053|WD=Q1145053|1=Kannō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1352|Q6112|WD=Q6112|1=1352}}), ``Munenobu'', who was a monk, and ``Hozawa'', a shrine priest, dedicated 13 carved wooden idols.

According to the shrine biography of Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, Sakamoto Shrine was relocated and became Suwa Shrine after its main building was destroyed by wildfire in {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}}2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}).

From this, it is assumed that Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine was established in {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), by order of {{ill|lt=Takeda Shingen|Q276404|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}, {{ill|lt=Kiso Yoshimasa|Q6416834|WD=Q6416834|1=Kiso Yoshimasa}} attacked {{ill|lt=Atera Castle|Q11657387|WD=Q11657387|1=Adera Castle}} and captured it, and the castle's lord, Toyama Tomoshige of {{ill|lt=Meisho Toyama clan|Q28682492|WD=Q28682492|1=Meisho Toyama clan}}, was killed in battle.

At that time, Sakamoto Shrine, Bettoji Temple, {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}, etc. were burned down, and the shrine buildings were lost.After that, if you are a vassal of {{ill|lt=Mr. Kiso|Q11518597|WD=Q11518597|1=Mr. Kiso}}, please refrain from internal links to strings that require disambiguation. There is no information. If you look carefully, the place name Shinano is not unique) --> Arashi Sanuki (Hikobei) who lived in Sendanbayashi Village <ref> There is a gravestone called ``Sanuki-sama'' in Sendanbayashi Koishizuka. It is a tombstone from the transitional period, when the five-ring pagoda, which was the main form of tombs in the Middle Ages, was transitioned to the pagoda, which is the main form of modern tombs. The tombstone is 1.2m high and 52cm wide, with the characters ``Sorafu, Fire, Water, Earth, and Earth'' engraved in the center, and the words ``Getsuoji Seizenjomon, 13th anniversary of death, Arashi Sanuki, Kan'ei 3rd year, Early Summer Anniversary, Takako, 8th son founding.'' The ruins of Arashi Sanuki's residence are located 200 meters west of this place, with the mountains in the background and the Fukasawa River in front, commonly known as ``Sanukido'' (Numajiri), where one of the five-ring towers still remains. The shrine building was rebuilt by </ref>'''.

In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was a senior vassal of {{ill|lt=Owari domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} and {{jalink|Yamamura magistrate's residence|Q55521349| It was renovated and restored with the cooperation of the villagers by the {{ill|lt=Kiso Shu#Yamamura Jinbei Family|Q86743699|WD=Q86743699|lt=Yamamura Jinbei Family|1=Yamamura Jinbei Family}}, who inherited the post of Kiso Magistrate}} and were also the lords of Sendanbayashi Village.

During the {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} and {{ill|lt=Shoyasu|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} years (1624-1648), the shrine priest ``Tairyuin Gondai Soto Kaiharu'' ruled over Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|WD=Q7584|1=1832}}), the current shrine was erected and reconstructed.

In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), the temple bell that had been there until then was moved to {{ill|lt=Dorinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dorinji|1=Dorinji}} due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance to Separate Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}.

In addition to this Hachiman Shrine, the Sakamoto Shrine, which is a Mino-koku-style shrine, is also known as {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine (Nasegawa, Nakatsugawa City)|Q11631918|WD=Q11631918|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine|1=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine}}.

There was a debate in the early days of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} as to whether the shrine called Sakamoto Shrine was Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine or Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, but no conclusion could be reached, and both shrines decided not to use the name Sakamoto Shrine, but to use ``Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Suwa Shrine'' as their official names. Currently, Hachiman Shrine is commonly called ``Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu'' and Suwa Shrine is ``Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine.''

Sakamoto Shrine was originally located on the site of Hachimangu Shrine, but it was burnt down between {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. The shrine that was relocated is called Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, and the one that was rebuilt on the site of the original Sakamoto Shrine is Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.

=== Bettoji Temple / Attached Temple ===

=== Ganjoji Temple ===
In the Edo period, in front of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the '''Daichizan of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} There used to be Ganjoji Temple, but it was closed down due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}} in the first year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), and ``Shofu School'' was established on its site in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). In Meiji 11 ({{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|WD=Q7776|1=1878}}) it was renamed "Sendanbayashi Elementary School", but in Meiji 34 ({{ill|lt=1901|Q2035|WD=Q2035|1=1901}}) it was moved to Nakahira.

=== Fumonin ===
Remains at Kumano Shrine in {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashichikuma District|Q1155105|WD=Q1155105|1=Higashichikuma District, Nagano}}{{ill|lt=Asahi Village|Q720461|WD=Q720461|1=Asahi, Nagano}}{{ja link|Waniguchi|Q11674315}} is engraved with {{Quotation|Minokuni, Toyamasho, Oigo, Sendanbayashi, Fumonin, Tanmei, Shinu, June 18th, Raikou, Keishaku}}.

Fumon-in was an affiliated temple that existed next to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine at the time, and it is described in the Shuzosho as 18 civilizations.

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinano Ogasawara clan|1=UNKNOWN}} occupied Waniguchi in {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} in 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646|1=1486}}), while occupying the central part of Ena District.

This indicates the fact that it was dedicated to Kumano Shrine in Asahi Village.

=== Zentoan ===
Mine Matsuyama {{ill|lt=Dairinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dairinji|1=Dairinji}} is said to have originally started as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 years ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}{{ill|lt=Koma County|Q11479803|WD=Q11479803|1=Koma District}}{{jalink |Nagasaka Town|Q6958592|Nagasaka}}'s {{ill|lt=Seikoji|Q85882028|WD=Q85882028|1=Seikoji}}'s fifth generation ``Genko Teruan'' came to this area to avoid the war, entered Bettoji Temple of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine, and built a hermitage called Zentoan in the east cave. Teruan Genko passed away at Zendo-an.

In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 8th year ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815|1=1622}}), the temple name was changed to Zento-ji Temple, and in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}10th year ({{ill|lt=1633|Q6870|WD=Q6870|1=1633}}), it was moved to the outside of Horinji Temple and became Dairinji Temple.

===Others===
In addition, it is written in the ``Mino Misakoshiki'' that there were other temples such as Daizen-in, Gyokuenbo, Shimonobo, Sembuku-in, Myo-on-an, Chozen-an, and Mandokoro.

The site of Daizen-in Temple is said to be near the current Yakushi-do Hall behind Tanaka, and the temple and shrine records record that Juo-do Hall was built on the site of Fumon-in Temple.

===Description in Mino Misakoshiki===
It was written in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}2 ({{ill|lt=1765|Q7665|WD=Q7665|1=1765}}), but the author is unknown.

===Designated cultural property===

===Nakatsugawa City Designated Cultural Property===
*Pair of wooden guardian dogs<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-06 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E7%8B%9B%E7%8A%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%AF%BE |title=Pair of wooden guardian dogs |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 55cm Kamakura period Designated date: March 24, 1968

It is a guardian dog facing sideways, and the coloring has faded. Since there is no inscription, the author and year are unknown. Since it is made of cypress wood parquet, it is estimated to have been made at the end of the Kamakura period.
*Wooden Goshintai Statue<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-04 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E5%BE%A1%E7%A5%9E%E4%BD%93%E5%83%8F |title=Wooden Goshintai Statue |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 20-33cm Muromachi period Designated date: March 24, 1968

Of the 11 statues of gods and 2 statues of monks, there are 6 statues of gods holding scepters, 10 statues with robes, and 9 statues with beards. All of the carvings are made of wood (single carving), and most of them are tall in shape. There are about six statues with an inscription dated ``1352'' on the bottom of the statue.

===Nakatsugawa City designated natural monument===
*Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-03-03 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%81%AE%E3%83%92%E3%83%88%E3%83%84%E3%83%90%E3%82%BF%E3%82%B4 |title=Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>

Eye circumference 1m, 0.84m Tree height 22m Designation date: October 28, 1971

{{ill|lt=Hitotsubatago|Q1073191|WD=Q1073191|1=Chionanthus retusus}} is a plant of the {{ill|lt=Oleander|Q1209922|WD=Q1209922|1=Osmanthus fragrans}} family, and its distribution in Japan is only in the middle reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}} and {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051|1=Tsushima Island}}. The four-lobed, thin, white flowers bloom around May, and when in full bloom they look like snow. Because it is a very rare and unfamiliar tree, it is also called the ``Nanjamonja tree''. This tree is divided into two trunks at the base.

===Surrounding Ruins===
There are many kiln ruins from the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period on the hill north of the Hachimangu forest of Sakamoto Shrine, some of which have been designated as historic sites by Gifu Prefecture.

Fragments of white porcelain pottery (yamachawan) from the Kamakura and Muromachi periods have been collected over a wide area, including this area.

In addition, the Five Ring Pagoda and the Hokyoin Pagoda can be seen in various places, and although it is located at a distance from Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the ruins of a medieval residence have been excavated from the Nishikagai Ruins, and related artifacts have also been unearthed.

=== Transportation ===
* About 2km from {{ill|lt=Chuo Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Minosakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}}
* {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kotsu|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|lt=Kitaena Bus|1=Kitaena Kotsu}}Get off at Sakamoto Misaka Line "Sendanbayashi" bus stop, then walk for 10 minutes
: This is different from the "Nakatsugawa Sendanbayashi Bus Stop" for express buses.

{{nihongo|'''Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu'''|坂本神社八幡宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mino Province]].

'''Hachimanjinja'' (Hachimanjinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa City|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}{{ill|lt=Sendanbayashi Village|Q22131707|WD=Q22131707|lt=Sendanbayashi|1=Sendanbayashi}}.

=== Enshrined God ===
*{{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
*{{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Obichuhiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
*{{ill|lt=Hachimangami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda Betsu no Mikoto|1=Hachiman}}

=== History ===
Originally, it was a place where {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} was enshrined to pray for safety on the {{ill|lt=Kanzaka Pass|Q11588847|WD=Q11588847|1=Misaka Pass}}.

It is said that in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the shrine was built with a solicitation from {{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}.

{{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} Sakamoto Shrine, {{ill|lt=Nakagawa Shrine (Nakatsugawa City)|Q11364920|WD=Q11364920|lt=Nakagawa Shrine|1=Nakagawa Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}} are listed.

Although the period is unknown, {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}{{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Hachimangu|1=Usa Jingū}} was promoted and enshrined there. It is said that around this time the name was changed to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

In the Heian period, {{ill|lt=Honji Suijaku|Q3080781|WD=Q3080781|1=Honji suijaku}} led to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=a mixture of Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was considered to be the same as {{ill|lt=Amida Nyorai|Q236242|WD=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}, and shrine priests controlled Shinto rituals.In {{ill|lt=Kanno|Q1145053|WD=Q1145053|1=Kannō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1352|Q6112|WD=Q6112|1=1352}}), ``Munenobu'', who was a monk, and ``Hozawa'', a shrine priest, dedicated 13 carved wooden idols.

According to the shrine biography of Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, Sakamoto Shrine was relocated and became Suwa Shrine after its main building was destroyed by wildfire in {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}}2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}).

From this, it is assumed that Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine was established in {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), by order of {{ill|lt=Takeda Shingen|Q276404|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}, {{ill|lt=Kiso Yoshimasa|Q6416834|WD=Q6416834|1=Kiso Yoshimasa}} attacked {{ill|lt=Atera Castle|Q11657387|WD=Q11657387|1=Adera Castle}} and captured it, and the castle's lord, Toyama Tomoshige of {{ill|lt=Meisho Toyama clan|Q28682492|WD=Q28682492|1=Meisho Toyama clan}}, was killed in battle.

At that time, Sakamoto Shrine, Bettoji Temple, {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}, etc. were burned down, and the shrine buildings were lost.After that, if you are a vassal of {{ill|lt=Mr. Kiso|Q11518597|WD=Q11518597|1=Mr. Kiso}}, please refrain from internal links to strings that require disambiguation. There is no information. If you look carefully, the place name Shinano is not unique) --> Arashi Sanuki (Hikobei) who lived in Sendanbayashi Village <ref> There is a gravestone called ``Sanuki-sama'' in Sendanbayashi Koishizuka. It is a tombstone from the transitional period, when the five-ring pagoda, which was the main form of tombs in the Middle Ages, was transitioned to the pagoda, which is the main form of modern tombs. The tombstone is 1.2m high and 52cm wide, with the characters ``Sorafu, Fire, Water, Earth, and Earth'' engraved in the center, and the words ``Getsuoji Seizenjomon, 13th anniversary of death, Arashi Sanuki, Kan'ei 3rd year, Early Summer Anniversary, Takako, 8th son founding.'' The ruins of Arashi Sanuki's residence are located 200 meters west of this place, with the mountains in the background and the Fukasawa River in front, commonly known as ``Sanukido'' (Numajiri), where one of the five-ring towers still remains. The shrine building was rebuilt by </ref>'''.

In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was a senior vassal of {{ill|lt=Owari domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} and {{jalink|Yamamura magistrate's residence|Q55521349| It was renovated and restored with the cooperation of the villagers by the {{ill|lt=Kiso Shu#Yamamura Jinbei Family|Q86743699|WD=Q86743699|lt=Yamamura Jinbei Family|1=Yamamura Jinbei Family}}, who inherited the post of Kiso Magistrate}} and were also the lords of Sendanbayashi Village.

During the {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} and {{ill|lt=Shoyasu|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} years (1624-1648), the shrine priest ``Tairyuin Gondai Soto Kaiharu'' ruled over Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|WD=Q7584|1=1832}}), the current shrine was erected and reconstructed.

In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), the temple bell that had been there until then was moved to {{ill|lt=Dorinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dorinji|1=Dorinji}} due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance to Separate Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}.

In addition to this Hachiman Shrine, the Sakamoto Shrine, which is a Mino-koku-style shrine, is also known as {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine (Nasegawa, Nakatsugawa City)|Q11631918|WD=Q11631918|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine|1=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine}}.

There was a debate in the early days of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} as to whether the shrine called Sakamoto Shrine was Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine or Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, but no conclusion could be reached, and both shrines decided not to use the name Sakamoto Shrine, but to use ``Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Suwa Shrine'' as their official names. Currently, Hachiman Shrine is commonly called ``Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu'' and Suwa Shrine is ``Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine.''

Sakamoto Shrine was originally located on the site of Hachimangu Shrine, but it was burnt down between {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. The shrine that was relocated is called Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, and the one that was rebuilt on the site of the original Sakamoto Shrine is Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.

=== Bettoji Temple / Attached Temple ===

=== Ganjoji Temple ===
In the Edo period, in front of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the '''Daichizan of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} There used to be Ganjoji Temple, but it was closed down due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}} in the first year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), and ``Shofu School'' was established on its site in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). In Meiji 11 ({{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|WD=Q7776|1=1878}}) it was renamed "Sendanbayashi Elementary School", but in Meiji 34 ({{ill|lt=1901|Q2035|WD=Q2035|1=1901}}) it was moved to Nakahira.

=== Fumonin ===
Remains at Kumano Shrine in {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashichikuma District|Q1155105|WD=Q1155105|1=Higashichikuma District, Nagano}}{{ill|lt=Asahi Village|Q720461|WD=Q720461|1=Asahi, Nagano}}{{ja link|Waniguchi|Q11674315}} is engraved with {{Quotation|Minokuni, Toyamasho, Oigo, Sendanbayashi, Fumonin, Tanmei, Shinu, June 18th, Raikou, Keishaku}}.

Fumon-in was an affiliated temple that existed next to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine at the time, and it is described in the Shuzosho as 18 civilizations.

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinano Ogasawara clan|1=UNKNOWN}} occupied Waniguchi in {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} in 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646|1=1486}}), while occupying the central part of Ena District.

This indicates the fact that it was dedicated to Kumano Shrine in Asahi Village.

=== Zentoan ===
Mine Matsuyama {{ill|lt=Dairinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dairinji|1=Dairinji}} is said to have originally started as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 years ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}{{ill|lt=Koma County|Q11479803|WD=Q11479803|1=Koma District}}{{jalink |Nagasaka Town|Q6958592|Nagasaka}}'s {{ill|lt=Seikoji|Q85882028|WD=Q85882028|1=Seikoji}}'s fifth generation ``Genko Teruan'' came to this area to avoid the war, entered Bettoji Temple of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine, and built a hermitage called Zentoan in the east cave. Teruan Genko passed away at Zendo-an.

In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 8th year ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815|1=1622}}), the temple name was changed to Zento-ji Temple, and in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}10th year ({{ill|lt=1633|Q6870|WD=Q6870|1=1633}}), it was moved to the outside of Horinji Temple and became Dairinji Temple.

===Others===
In addition, it is written in the ``Mino Misakoshiki'' that there were other temples such as Daizen-in, Gyokuenbo, Shimonobo, Sembuku-in, Myo-on-an, Chozen-an, and Mandokoro.

The site of Daizen-in Temple is said to be near the current Yakushi-do Hall behind Tanaka, and the temple and shrine records record that Juo-do Hall was built on the site of Fumon-in Temple.

===Description in Mino Misakoshiki===
It was written in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}2 ({{ill|lt=1765|Q7665|WD=Q7665|1=1765}}), but the author is unknown.

===Designated cultural property===

===Nakatsugawa City Designated Cultural Property===
*Pair of wooden guardian dogs<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-06 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E7%8B%9B%E7%8A%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%AF%BE |title=Pair of wooden guardian dogs |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 55cm Kamakura period Designated date: March 24, 1968

It is a guardian dog facing sideways, and the coloring has faded. Since there is no inscription, the author and year are unknown. Since it is made of cypress wood parquet, it is estimated to have been made at the end of the Kamakura period.
*Wooden Goshintai Statue<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-04 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E5%BE%A1%E7%A5%9E%E4%BD%93%E5%83%8F |title=Wooden Goshintai Statue |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 20-33cm Muromachi period Designated date: March 24, 1968

Of the 11 statues of gods and 2 statues of monks, there are 6 statues of gods holding scepters, 10 statues with robes, and 9 statues with beards. All of the carvings are made of wood (single carving), and most of them are tall in shape. There are about six statues with an inscription dated ``1352'' on the bottom of the statue.

===Nakatsugawa City designated natural monument===
*Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-03-03 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%81%AE%E3%83%92%E3%83%88%E3%83%84%E3%83%90%E3%82%BF%E3%82%B4 |title=Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>

Eye circumference 1m, 0.84m Tree height 22m Designation date: October 28, 1971

{{ill|lt=Hitotsubatago|Q1073191|WD=Q1073191|1=Chionanthus retusus}} is a plant of the {{ill|lt=Oleander|Q1209922|WD=Q1209922|1=Osmanthus fragrans}} family, and its distribution in Japan is only in the middle reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}} and {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051|1=Tsushima Island}}. The four-lobed, thin, white flowers bloom around May, and when in full bloom they look like snow. Because it is a very rare and unfamiliar tree, it is also called the ``Nanjamonja tree''. This tree is divided into two trunks at the base.

===Surrounding Ruins===
There are many kiln ruins from the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period on the hill north of the Hachimangu forest of Sakamoto Shrine, some of which have been designated as historic sites by Gifu Prefecture.

Fragments of white porcelain pottery (yamachawan) from the Kamakura and Muromachi periods have been collected over a wide area, including this area.

In addition, the Five Ring Pagoda and the Hokyoin Pagoda can be seen in various places, and although it is located at a distance from Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the ruins of a medieval residence have been excavated from the Nishikagai Ruins, and related artifacts have also been unearthed.

=== Transportation ===
* About 2km from {{ill|lt=Chuo Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Minosakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}}
* {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kotsu|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|lt=Kitaena Bus|1=Kitaena Kotsu}}Get off at Sakamoto Misaka Line "Sendanbayashi" bus stop, then walk for 10 minutes
: This is different from the "Nakatsugawa Sendanbayashi Bus Stop" for express buses.

{{nihongo|'''Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu'''|坂本神社八幡宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mino Province]].

'''Hachimanjinja'' (Hachimanjinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa City|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}{{ill|lt=Sendanbayashi Village|Q22131707|WD=Q22131707|lt=Sendanbayashi|1=Sendanbayashi}}.

=== Enshrined God ===
*{{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
*{{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Obichuhiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
*{{ill|lt=Hachimangami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda Betsu no Mikoto|1=Hachiman}}

=== History ===
Originally, it was a place where {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} was enshrined to pray for safety on the {{ill|lt=Kanzaka Pass|Q11588847|WD=Q11588847|1=Misaka Pass}}.

It is said that in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the shrine was built with a solicitation from {{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}.

{{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} Sakamoto Shrine, {{ill|lt=Nakagawa Shrine (Nakatsugawa City)|Q11364920|WD=Q11364920|lt=Nakagawa Shrine|1=Nakagawa Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}} are listed.

Although the period is unknown, {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}{{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Hachimangu|1=Usa Jingū}} was promoted and enshrined there. It is said that around this time the name was changed to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

In the Heian period, {{ill|lt=Honji Suijaku|Q3080781|WD=Q3080781|1=Honji suijaku}} led to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=a mixture of Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was considered to be the same as {{ill|lt=Amida Nyorai|Q236242|WD=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}, and shrine priests controlled Shinto rituals.In {{ill|lt=Kanno|Q1145053|WD=Q1145053|1=Kannō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1352|Q6112|WD=Q6112|1=1352}}), ``Munenobu'', who was a monk, and ``Hozawa'', a shrine priest, dedicated 13 carved wooden idols.

According to the shrine biography of Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, Sakamoto Shrine was relocated and became Suwa Shrine after its main building was destroyed by wildfire in {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}}2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}).

From this, it is assumed that Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine was established in {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), by order of {{ill|lt=Takeda Shingen|Q276404|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}, {{ill|lt=Kiso Yoshimasa|Q6416834|WD=Q6416834|1=Kiso Yoshimasa}} attacked {{ill|lt=Atera Castle|Q11657387|WD=Q11657387|1=Adera Castle}} and captured it, and the castle's lord, Toyama Tomoshige of {{ill|lt=Meisho Toyama clan|Q28682492|WD=Q28682492|1=Meisho Toyama clan}}, was killed in battle.

At that time, Sakamoto Shrine, Bettoji Temple, {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}, etc. were burned down, and the shrine buildings were lost.After that, if you are a vassal of {{ill|lt=Mr. Kiso|Q11518597|WD=Q11518597|1=Mr. Kiso}}, please refrain from internal links to strings that require disambiguation. There is no information. If you look carefully, the place name Shinano is not unique) --> Arashi Sanuki (Hikobei) who lived in Sendanbayashi Village <ref> There is a gravestone called ``Sanuki-sama'' in Sendanbayashi Koishizuka. It is a tombstone from the transitional period, when the five-ring pagoda, which was the main form of tombs in the Middle Ages, was transitioned to the pagoda, which is the main form of modern tombs. The tombstone is 1.2m high and 52cm wide, with the characters ``Sorafu, Fire, Water, Earth, and Earth'' engraved in the center, and the words ``Getsuoji Seizenjomon, 13th anniversary of death, Arashi Sanuki, Kan'ei 3rd year, Early Summer Anniversary, Takako, 8th son founding.'' The ruins of Arashi Sanuki's residence are located 200 meters west of this place, with the mountains in the background and the Fukasawa River in front, commonly known as ``Sanukido'' (Numajiri), where one of the five-ring towers still remains. The shrine building was rebuilt by </ref>'''.

In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was a senior vassal of {{ill|lt=Owari domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} and {{jalink|Yamamura magistrate's residence|Q55521349| It was renovated and restored with the cooperation of the villagers by the {{ill|lt=Kiso Shu#Yamamura Jinbei Family|Q86743699|WD=Q86743699|lt=Yamamura Jinbei Family|1=Yamamura Jinbei Family}}, who inherited the post of Kiso Magistrate}} and were also the lords of Sendanbayashi Village.

During the {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} and {{ill|lt=Shoyasu|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} years (1624-1648), the shrine priest ``Tairyuin Gondai Soto Kaiharu'' ruled over Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|WD=Q7584|1=1832}}), the current shrine was erected and reconstructed.

In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), the temple bell that had been there until then was moved to {{ill|lt=Dorinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dorinji|1=Dorinji}} due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance to Separate Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}.

In addition to this Hachiman Shrine, the Sakamoto Shrine, which is a Mino-koku-style shrine, is also known as {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine (Nasegawa, Nakatsugawa City)|Q11631918|WD=Q11631918|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine|1=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine}}.

There was a debate in the early days of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} as to whether the shrine called Sakamoto Shrine was Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine or Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, but no conclusion could be reached, and both shrines decided not to use the name Sakamoto Shrine, but to use ``Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Suwa Shrine'' as their official names. Currently, Hachiman Shrine is commonly called ``Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu'' and Suwa Shrine is ``Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine.''

Sakamoto Shrine was originally located on the site of Hachimangu Shrine, but it was burnt down between {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. The shrine that was relocated is called Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, and the one that was rebuilt on the site of the original Sakamoto Shrine is Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.

=== Bettoji Temple / Attached Temple ===

=== Ganjoji Temple ===
In the Edo period, in front of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the '''Daichizan of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} There used to be Ganjoji Temple, but it was closed down due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}} in the first year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), and ``Shofu School'' was established on its site in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). In Meiji 11 ({{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|WD=Q7776|1=1878}}) it was renamed "Sendanbayashi Elementary School", but in Meiji 34 ({{ill|lt=1901|Q2035|WD=Q2035|1=1901}}) it was moved to Nakahira.

=== Fumonin ===
Remains at Kumano Shrine in {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashichikuma District|Q1155105|WD=Q1155105|1=Higashichikuma District, Nagano}}{{ill|lt=Asahi Village|Q720461|WD=Q720461|1=Asahi, Nagano}}{{ja link|Waniguchi|Q11674315}} is engraved with {{Quotation|Minokuni, Toyamasho, Oigo, Sendanbayashi, Fumonin, Tanmei, Shinu, June 18th, Raikou, Keishaku}}.

Fumon-in was an affiliated temple that existed next to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine at the time, and it is described in the Shuzosho as 18 civilizations.

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinano Ogasawara clan|1=UNKNOWN}} occupied Waniguchi in {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} in 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646|1=1486}}), while occupying the central part of Ena District.

This indicates the fact that it was dedicated to Kumano Shrine in Asahi Village.

=== Zentoan ===
Mine Matsuyama {{ill|lt=Dairinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dairinji|1=Dairinji}} is said to have originally started as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 years ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}{{ill|lt=Koma County|Q11479803|WD=Q11479803|1=Koma District}}{{jalink |Nagasaka Town|Q6958592|Nagasaka}}'s {{ill|lt=Seikoji|Q85882028|WD=Q85882028|1=Seikoji}}'s fifth generation ``Genko Teruan'' came to this area to avoid the war, entered Bettoji Temple of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine, and built a hermitage called Zentoan in the east cave. Teruan Genko passed away at Zendo-an.

In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 8th year ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815|1=1622}}), the temple name was changed to Zento-ji Temple, and in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}10th year ({{ill|lt=1633|Q6870|WD=Q6870|1=1633}}), it was moved to the outside of Horinji Temple and became Dairinji Temple.

===Others===
In addition, it is written in the ``Mino Misakoshiki'' that there were other temples such as Daizen-in, Gyokuenbo, Shimonobo, Sembuku-in, Myo-on-an, Chozen-an, and Mandokoro.

The site of Daizen-in Temple is said to be near the current Yakushi-do Hall behind Tanaka, and the temple and shrine records record that Juo-do Hall was built on the site of Fumon-in Temple.

===Designated cultural property===

===Nakatsugawa City Designated Cultural Property===
*Pair of wooden guardian dogs<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-06 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E7%8B%9B%E7%8A%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%AF%BE |title=Pair of wooden guardian dogs |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 55cm Kamakura period Designated date: March 24, 1968

It is a guardian dog facing sideways, and the coloring has faded. Since there is no inscription, the author and year are unknown. Since it is made of cypress wood parquet, it is estimated to have been made at the end of the Kamakura period.
*Wooden Goshintai Statue<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-04 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E5%BE%A1%E7%A5%9E%E4%BD%93%E5%83%8F |title=Wooden Goshintai Statue |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 20-33cm Muromachi period Designated date: March 24, 1968

Of the 11 statues of gods and 2 statues of monks, there are 6 statues of gods holding scepters, 10 statues with robes, and 9 statues with beards. All of the carvings are made of wood (single carving), and most of them are tall in shape. There are about six statues with an inscription dated ``1352'' on the bottom of the statue.

===Nakatsugawa City designated natural monument===
*Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-03-03 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%81%AE%E3%83%92%E3%83%88%E3%83%84%E3%83%90%E3%82%BF%E3%82%B4 |title=Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>

Eye circumference 1m, 0.84m Tree height 22m Designation date: October 28, 1971

{{ill|lt=Hitotsubatago|Q1073191|WD=Q1073191|1=Chionanthus retusus}} is a plant of the {{ill|lt=Oleander|Q1209922|WD=Q1209922|1=Osmanthus fragrans}} family, and its distribution in Japan is only in the middle reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}} and {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051|1=Tsushima Island}}. The four-lobed, thin, white flowers bloom around May, and when in full bloom they look like snow. Because it is a very rare and unfamiliar tree, it is also called the ``Nanjamonja tree''. This tree is divided into two trunks at the base.

===Surrounding Ruins===
There are many kiln ruins from the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period on the hill north of the Hachimangu forest of Sakamoto Shrine, some of which have been designated as historic sites by Gifu Prefecture.

Fragments of white porcelain pottery (yamachawan) from the Kamakura and Muromachi periods have been collected over a wide area, including this area.

In addition, the Five Ring Pagoda and the Hokyoin Pagoda can be seen in various places, and although it is located at a distance from Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the ruins of a medieval residence have been excavated from the Nishikagai Ruins, and related artifacts have also been unearthed.

=== Transportation ===

: This is different from the "Nakatsugawa Sendanbayashi Bus Stop" for express buses.

{{nihongo|'''Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu'''|坂本神社八幡宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mino Province]].

'''Hachimanjinja'' (Hachimanjinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa City|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}{{ill|lt=Sendanbayashi Village|Q22131707|WD=Q22131707|lt=Sendanbayashi|1=Sendanbayashi}}.

=== Enshrined God ===
*{{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
*{{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Obichuhiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
*{{ill|lt=Hachimangami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda Betsu no Mikoto|1=Hachiman}}

=== History ===
Originally, it was a place where {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} was enshrined to pray for safety on the {{ill|lt=Kanzaka Pass|Q11588847|WD=Q11588847|1=Misaka Pass}}.

It is said that in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the shrine was built with a solicitation from {{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}.

{{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} Sakamoto Shrine, {{ill|lt=Nakagawa Shrine (Nakatsugawa City)|Q11364920|WD=Q11364920|lt=Nakagawa Shrine|1=Nakagawa Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}} are listed.

Although the period is unknown, {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}{{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Hachimangu|1=Usa Jingū}} was promoted and enshrined there. It is said that around this time the name was changed to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

In the Heian period, {{ill|lt=Honji Suijaku|Q3080781|WD=Q3080781|1=Honji suijaku}} led to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=a mixture of Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was considered to be the same as {{ill|lt=Amida Nyorai|Q236242|WD=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}, and shrine priests controlled Shinto rituals.In {{ill|lt=Kanno|Q1145053|WD=Q1145053|1=Kannō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1352|Q6112|WD=Q6112|1=1352}}), ``Munenobu'', who was a monk, and ``Hozawa'', a shrine priest, dedicated 13 carved wooden idols.

According to the shrine biography of Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, Sakamoto Shrine was relocated and became Suwa Shrine after its main building was destroyed by wildfire in {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}}2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}).

From this, it is assumed that Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine was established in {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), by order of {{ill|lt=Takeda Shingen|Q276404|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}, {{ill|lt=Kiso Yoshimasa|Q6416834|WD=Q6416834|1=Kiso Yoshimasa}} attacked {{ill|lt=Atera Castle|Q11657387|WD=Q11657387|1=Adera Castle}} and captured it, and the castle's lord, Toyama Tomoshige of {{ill|lt=Meisho Toyama clan|Q28682492|WD=Q28682492|1=Meisho Toyama clan}}, was killed in battle.

At that time, Sakamoto Shrine, Bettoji Temple, {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}, etc. were burned down, and the shrine buildings were lost.After that, if you are a vassal of {{ill|lt=Mr. Kiso|Q11518597|WD=Q11518597|1=Mr. Kiso}}, please refrain from internal links to strings that require disambiguation. There is no information. If you look carefully, the place name Shinano is not unique) --> Arashi Sanuki (Hikobei) who lived in Sendanbayashi Village <ref> There is a gravestone called ``Sanuki-sama'' in Sendanbayashi Koishizuka. It is a tombstone from the transitional period, when the five-ring pagoda, which was the main form of tombs in the Middle Ages, was transitioned to the pagoda, which is the main form of modern tombs. The tombstone is 1.2m high and 52cm wide, with the characters ``Sorafu, Fire, Water, Earth, and Earth'' engraved in the center, and the words ``Getsuoji Seizenjomon, 13th anniversary of death, Arashi Sanuki, Kan'ei 3rd year, Early Summer Anniversary, Takako, 8th son founding.'' The ruins of Arashi Sanuki's residence are located 200 meters west of this place, with the mountains in the background and the Fukasawa River in front, commonly known as ``Sanukido'' (Numajiri), where one of the five-ring towers still remains. The shrine building was rebuilt by </ref>'''.

In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was a senior vassal of {{ill|lt=Owari domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} and {{jalink|Yamamura magistrate's residence|Q55521349| It was renovated and restored with the cooperation of the villagers by the {{ill|lt=Kiso Shu#Yamamura Jinbei Family|Q86743699|WD=Q86743699|lt=Yamamura Jinbei Family|1=Yamamura Jinbei Family}}, who inherited the post of Kiso Magistrate}} and were also the lords of Sendanbayashi Village.

During the {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} and {{ill|lt=Shoyasu|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} years (1624-1648), the shrine priest ``Tairyuin Gondai Soto Kaiharu'' ruled over Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|WD=Q7584|1=1832}}), the current shrine was erected and reconstructed.

In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), the temple bell that had been there until then was moved to {{ill|lt=Dorinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dorinji|1=Dorinji}} due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance to Separate Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}.

In addition to this Hachiman Shrine, the Sakamoto Shrine, which is a Mino-koku-style shrine, is also known as {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine (Nasegawa, Nakatsugawa City)|Q11631918|WD=Q11631918|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine|1=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine}}.

There was a debate in the early days of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} as to whether the shrine called Sakamoto Shrine was Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine or Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, but no conclusion could be reached, and both shrines decided not to use the name Sakamoto Shrine, but to use ``Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Suwa Shrine'' as their official names. Currently, Hachiman Shrine is commonly called ``Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu'' and Suwa Shrine is ``Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine.''

Sakamoto Shrine was originally located on the site of Hachimangu Shrine, but it was burnt down between {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. The shrine that was relocated is called Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, and the one that was rebuilt on the site of the original Sakamoto Shrine is Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.

=== Bettoji Temple / Attached Temple ===

=== Ganjoji Temple ===
In the Edo period, in front of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the '''Daichizan of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} There used to be Ganjoji Temple, but it was closed down due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}} in the first year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), and ``Shofu School'' was established on its site in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). In Meiji 11 ({{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|WD=Q7776|1=1878}}) it was renamed "Sendanbayashi Elementary School", but in Meiji 34 ({{ill|lt=1901|Q2035|WD=Q2035|1=1901}}) it was moved to Nakahira.

=== Fumonin ===
Remains at Kumano Shrine in {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashichikuma District|Q1155105|WD=Q1155105|1=Higashichikuma District, Nagano}}{{ill|lt=Asahi Village|Q720461|WD=Q720461|1=Asahi, Nagano}}{{ja link|Waniguchi|Q11674315}} is engraved with {{Quotation|Minokuni, Toyamasho, Oigo, Sendanbayashi, Fumonin, Tanmei, Shinu, June 18th, Raikou, Keishaku}}.

Fumon-in was an affiliated temple that existed next to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine at the time, and it is described in the Shuzosho as 18 civilizations.

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinano Ogasawara clan|1=UNKNOWN}} occupied Waniguchi in {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} in 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646|1=1486}}), while occupying the central part of Ena District.

This indicates the fact that it was dedicated to Kumano Shrine in Asahi Village.

=== Zentoan ===
Mine Matsuyama {{ill|lt=Dairinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dairinji|1=Dairinji}} is said to have originally started as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 years ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}{{ill|lt=Koma County|Q11479803|WD=Q11479803|1=Koma District}}{{jalink |Nagasaka Town|Q6958592|Nagasaka}}'s {{ill|lt=Seikoji|Q85882028|WD=Q85882028|1=Seikoji}}'s fifth generation ``Genko Teruan'' came to this area to avoid the war, entered Bettoji Temple of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine, and built a hermitage called Zentoan in the east cave. Teruan Genko passed away at Zendo-an.

In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 8th year ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815|1=1622}}), the temple name was changed to Zento-ji Temple, and in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}10th year ({{ill|lt=1633|Q6870|WD=Q6870|1=1633}}), it was moved to the outside of Horinji Temple and became Dairinji Temple.

===Others===
In addition, it is written in the ``Mino Misakoshiki'' that there were other temples such as Daizen-in, Gyokuenbo, Shimonobo, Sembuku-in, Myo-on-an, Chozen-an, and Mandokoro.

The site of Daizen-in Temple is said to be near the current Yakushi-do Hall behind Tanaka, and the temple and shrine records record that Juo-do Hall was built on the site of Fumon-in Temple.

===Designated cultural property===

===Nakatsugawa City Designated Cultural Property===
*Pair of wooden guardian dogs<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-06 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E7%8B%9B%E7%8A%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%AF%BE |title=Pair of wooden guardian dogs |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 55cm Kamakura period Designated date: March 24, 1968

It is a guardian dog facing sideways, and the coloring has faded. Since there is no inscription, the author and year are unknown. Since it is made of cypress wood parquet, it is estimated to have been made at the end of the Kamakura period.
*Wooden Goshintai Statue<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-04 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E5%BE%A1%E7%A5%9E%E4%BD%93%E5%83%8F |title=Wooden Goshintai Statue |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 20-33cm Muromachi period Designated date: March 24, 1968

Of the 11 statues of gods and 2 statues of monks, there are 6 statues of gods holding scepters, 10 statues with robes, and 9 statues with beards. All of the carvings are made of wood (single carving), and most of them are tall in shape. There are about six statues with an inscription dated ``1352'' on the bottom of the statue.

===Nakatsugawa City designated natural monument===
*Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-03-03 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%81%AE%E3%83%92%E3%83%88%E3%83%84%E3%83%90%E3%82%BF%E3%82%B4 |title=Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>

Eye circumference 1m, 0.84m Tree height 22m Designation date: October 28, 1971

{{ill|lt=Hitotsubatago|Q1073191|WD=Q1073191|1=Chionanthus retusus}} is a plant of the {{ill|lt=Oleander|Q1209922|WD=Q1209922|1=Osmanthus fragrans}} family, and its distribution in Japan is only in the middle reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}} and {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051|1=Tsushima Island}}. The four-lobed, thin, white flowers bloom around May, and when in full bloom they look like snow. Because it is a very rare and unfamiliar tree, it is also called the ``Nanjamonja tree''. This tree is divided into two trunks at the base.

===Surrounding Ruins===
There are many kiln ruins from the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period on the hill north of the Hachimangu forest of Sakamoto Shrine, some of which have been designated as historic sites by Gifu Prefecture.

Fragments of white porcelain pottery (yamachawan) from the Kamakura and Muromachi periods have been collected over a wide area, including this area.

In addition, the Five Ring Pagoda and the Hokyoin Pagoda can be seen in various places, and although it is located at a distance from Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the ruins of a medieval residence have been excavated from the Nishikagai Ruins, and related artifacts have also been unearthed.

=== Transportation ===
* About 2km from {{ill|lt=Chuo Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Minosakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}}
* {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kotsu|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|lt=Kitaena Bus|1=Kitaena Kotsu}}Get off at Sakamoto Misaka Line "Sendanbayashi" bus stop, then walk for 10 minutes
: This is different from the "Nakatsugawa Sendanbayashi Bus Stop" for express buses.

'''Hachimanjinja'' (Hachimanjinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa City|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}{{ill|lt=Sendanbayashi Village|Q22131707|WD=Q22131707|lt=Sendanbayashi|1=Sendanbayashi}}.

=== Enshrined God ===
*{{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
*{{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Obichuhiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
*{{ill|lt=Hachimangami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda Betsu no Mikoto|1=Hachiman}}

=== History ===
Originally, it was a place where {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamagi Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}} was enshrined to pray for safety on the {{ill|lt=Kanzaka Pass|Q11588847|WD=Q11588847|1=Misaka Pass}}.

It is said that in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the shrine was built with a solicitation from {{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}.

{{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} Sakamoto Shrine, {{ill|lt=Nakagawa Shrine (Nakatsugawa City)|Q11364920|WD=Q11364920|lt=Nakagawa Shrine|1=Nakagawa Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}} are listed.

Although the period is unknown, {{ill|lt=Buzen Province|Q1047134|WD=Q1047134|1=Buzen Province}}{{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Hachimangu|1=Usa Jingū}} was promoted and enshrined there. It is said that around this time the name was changed to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

In the Heian period, {{ill|lt=Honji Suijaku|Q3080781|WD=Q3080781|1=Honji suijaku}} led to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=a mixture of Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was considered to be the same as {{ill|lt=Amida Nyorai|Q236242|WD=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}, and shrine priests controlled Shinto rituals.In {{ill|lt=Kanno|Q1145053|WD=Q1145053|1=Kannō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1352|Q6112|WD=Q6112|1=1352}}), ``Munenobu'', who was a monk, and ``Hozawa'', a shrine priest, dedicated 13 carved wooden idols.

According to the shrine biography of Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, Sakamoto Shrine was relocated and became Suwa Shrine after its main building was destroyed by wildfire in {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}}2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}).

From this, it is assumed that Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine was established in {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), by order of {{ill|lt=Takeda Shingen|Q276404|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}, {{ill|lt=Kiso Yoshimasa|Q6416834|WD=Q6416834|1=Kiso Yoshimasa}} attacked {{ill|lt=Atera Castle|Q11657387|WD=Q11657387|1=Adera Castle}} and captured it, and the castle's lord, Toyama Tomoshige of {{ill|lt=Meisho Toyama clan|Q28682492|WD=Q28682492|1=Meisho Toyama clan}}, was killed in battle.

At that time, Sakamoto Shrine, Bettoji Temple, {{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}, etc. were burned down, and the shrine buildings were lost.After that, if you are a vassal of {{ill|lt=Mr. Kiso|Q11518597|WD=Q11518597|1=Mr. Kiso}}, please refrain from internal links to strings that require disambiguation. There is no information. If you look carefully, the place name Shinano is not unique) --> Arashi Sanuki (Hikobei) who lived in Sendanbayashi Village <ref> There is a gravestone called ``Sanuki-sama'' in Sendanbayashi Koishizuka. It is a tombstone from the transitional period, when the five-ring pagoda, which was the main form of tombs in the Middle Ages, was transitioned to the pagoda, which is the main form of modern tombs. The tombstone is 1.2m high and 52cm wide, with the characters ``Sorafu, Fire, Water, Earth, and Earth'' engraved in the center, and the words ``Getsuoji Seizenjomon, 13th anniversary of death, Arashi Sanuki, Kan'ei 3rd year, Early Summer Anniversary, Takako, 8th son founding.'' The ruins of Arashi Sanuki's residence are located 200 meters west of this place, with the mountains in the background and the Fukasawa River in front, commonly known as ``Sanukido'' (Numajiri), where one of the five-ring towers still remains. The shrine building was rebuilt by </ref>'''.

In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was a senior vassal of {{ill|lt=Owari domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} and {{jalink|Yamamura magistrate's residence|Q55521349| It was renovated and restored with the cooperation of the villagers by the {{ill|lt=Kiso Shu#Yamamura Jinbei Family|Q86743699|WD=Q86743699|lt=Yamamura Jinbei Family|1=Yamamura Jinbei Family}}, who inherited the post of Kiso Magistrate}} and were also the lords of Sendanbayashi Village.

During the {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} and {{ill|lt=Shoyasu|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} years (1624-1648), the shrine priest ``Tairyuin Gondai Soto Kaiharu'' ruled over Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|WD=Q7584|1=1832}}), the current shrine was erected and reconstructed.

In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), the temple bell that had been there until then was moved to {{ill|lt=Dorinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dorinji|1=Dorinji}} due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance to Separate Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}.

In addition to this Hachiman Shrine, the Sakamoto Shrine, which is a Mino-koku-style shrine, is also known as {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine (Nasegawa, Nakatsugawa City)|Q11631918|WD=Q11631918|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine|1=Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine}}.

There was a debate in the early days of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} as to whether the shrine called Sakamoto Shrine was Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine or Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, but no conclusion could be reached, and both shrines decided not to use the name Sakamoto Shrine, but to use ``Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Suwa Shrine'' as their official names. Currently, Hachiman Shrine is commonly called ``Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu'' and Suwa Shrine is ``Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine.''

Sakamoto Shrine was originally located on the site of Hachimangu Shrine, but it was burnt down between {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. The shrine that was relocated is called Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine, and the one that was rebuilt on the site of the original Sakamoto Shrine is Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine.

=== Bettoji Temple / Attached Temple ===

=== Ganjoji Temple ===
In the Edo period, in front of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the '''Daichizan of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} There used to be Ganjoji Temple, but it was closed down due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}} in the first year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), and ``Shofu School'' was established on its site in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). In Meiji 11 ({{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|WD=Q7776|1=1878}}) it was renamed "Sendanbayashi Elementary School", but in Meiji 34 ({{ill|lt=1901|Q2035|WD=Q2035|1=1901}}) it was moved to Nakahira.

=== Fumonin ===
Remains at Kumano Shrine in {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashichikuma District|Q1155105|WD=Q1155105|1=Higashichikuma District, Nagano}}{{ill|lt=Asahi Village|Q720461|WD=Q720461|1=Asahi, Nagano}}{{ja link|Waniguchi|Q11674315}} is engraved with {{Quotation|Minokuni, Toyamasho, Oigo, Sendanbayashi, Fumonin, Tanmei, Shinu, June 18th, Raikou, Keishaku}}.

Fumon-in was an affiliated temple that existed next to Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine at the time, and it is described in the Shuzosho as 18 civilizations.

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinano Ogasawara clan|1=UNKNOWN}} occupied Waniguchi in {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} in 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646|1=1486}}), while occupying the central part of Ena District.

This indicates the fact that it was dedicated to Kumano Shrine in Asahi Village.

=== Zentoan ===
Mine Matsuyama {{ill|lt=Dairinji (Nakatsugawa City)|Q60989611|WD=Q60989611|lt=Dairinji|1=Dairinji}} is said to have originally started as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine.

{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 years ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}{{ill|lt=Koma County|Q11479803|WD=Q11479803|1=Koma District}}{{jalink |Nagasaka Town|Q6958592|Nagasaka}}'s {{ill|lt=Seikoji|Q85882028|WD=Q85882028|1=Seikoji}}'s fifth generation ``Genko Teruan'' came to this area to avoid the war, entered Bettoji Temple of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman Shrine, and built a hermitage called Zentoan in the east cave. Teruan Genko passed away at Zendo-an.

In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 8th year ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815|1=1622}}), the temple name was changed to Zento-ji Temple, and in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}10th year ({{ill|lt=1633|Q6870|WD=Q6870|1=1633}}), it was moved to the outside of Horinji Temple and became Dairinji Temple.

===Others===
In addition, it is written in the ``Mino Misakoshiki'' that there were other temples such as Daizen-in, Gyokuenbo, Shimonobo, Sembuku-in, Myo-on-an, Chozen-an, and Mandokoro.

The site of Daizen-in Temple is said to be near the current Yakushi-do Hall behind Tanaka, and the temple and shrine records record that Juo-do Hall was built on the site of Fumon-in Temple.

===Description in Mino Misakoshiki===
It was written in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}2 ({{ill|lt=1765|Q7665|WD=Q7665|1=1765}}), but the author is unknown.

===Designated cultural property===

===Nakatsugawa City Designated Cultural Property===
*Pair of wooden guardian dogs<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-06 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E7%8B%9B%E7%8A%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%AF%BE |title=Pair of wooden guardian dogs |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 55cm Kamakura period Designated date: March 24, 1968

It is a guardian dog facing sideways, and the coloring has faded. Since there is no inscription, the author and year are unknown. Since it is made of cypress wood parquet, it is estimated to have been made at the end of the Kamakura period.
*Wooden Goshintai Statue<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-01-04 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E9%80%A0%E5%BE%A1%E7%A5%9E%E4%BD%93%E5%83%8F |title=Wooden Goshintai Statue |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>
Statue height 20-33cm Muromachi period Designated date: March 24, 1968

Of the 11 statues of gods and 2 statues of monks, there are 6 statues of gods holding scepters, 10 statues with robes, and 9 statues with beards. All of the carvings are made of wood (single carving), and most of them are tall in shape. There are about six statues with an inscription dated ``1352'' on the bottom of the statue.

===Nakatsugawa City designated natural monument===
*Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2011-03-03 |url=http://www.city.nakatsugawa.gifu.jp/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%81%AE%E3%83%92%E3%83%88%E3%83%84%E3%83%90%E3%82%BF%E3%82%B4 |title=Hitotsubatago of Yawata Shrine |publisher=Nakatsugawa City |accessdate=2013-04-24}}</ref>

Eye circumference 1m, 0.84m Tree height 22m Designation date: October 28, 1971

{{ill|lt=Hitotsubatago|Q1073191|WD=Q1073191|1=Chionanthus retusus}} is a plant of the {{ill|lt=Oleander|Q1209922|WD=Q1209922|1=Osmanthus fragrans}} family, and its distribution in Japan is only in the middle reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}} and {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051|1=Tsushima Island}}. The four-lobed, thin, white flowers bloom around May, and when in full bloom they look like snow. Because it is a very rare and unfamiliar tree, it is also called the ``Nanjamonja tree''. This tree is divided into two trunks at the base.

===Surrounding Ruins===
There are many kiln ruins from the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period on the hill north of the Hachimangu forest of Sakamoto Shrine, some of which have been designated as historic sites by Gifu Prefecture.

Fragments of white porcelain pottery (yamachawan) from the Kamakura and Muromachi periods have been collected over a wide area, including this area.

In addition, the Five Ring Pagoda and the Hokyoin Pagoda can be seen in various places, and although it is located at a distance from Sakamoto Shrine Hachimangu Shrine, the ruins of a medieval residence have been excavated from the Nishikagai Ruins, and related artifacts have also been unearthed.

=== Transportation ===
* About 2km from {{ill|lt=Chuo Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Minosakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}}
* {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kotsu|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|lt=Kitaena Bus|1=Kitaena Kotsu}}Get off at Sakamoto Misaka Line "Sendanbayashi" bus stop, then walk for 10 minutes
: This is different from the "Nakatsugawa Sendanbayashi Bus Stop" for express buses.

{{nihongo|'''Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu'''|坂本神社八幡宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mino Province]].
